narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, an LSAT score of 32.50, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 4th (below the typical 5th to 6th decile averages), and coming from an upper-middle family income bracket, with plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 49.1%—a risk level that unfortunately tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize those with stronger predicted odds of passing. Your undergraduate GPA emerged as the single most influential factor, actually working in your favor by suggesting a somewhat better chance of passing than the model might expect from even lower scores. However, your family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward failure risk compared to lower-income peers who sometimes outperform expectations. Your Year 3 decile rank then played a key role in pushing toward higher failure odds, as it sits below the dataset average and signals middling performance relative to classmates. Similarly, your Year 1 decile contributed in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic standing compared to typical students. Your LSAT score followed as another factor leaning toward failure risk, given it's under the average of 36.7 seen in the data. On a brighter note, planning to attend full-time helped pull the prediction toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students who pass. Overall, while no single element doomed your application, the combination of below-average deciles and LSAT outweighed the positives like your GPA and full-time status when stacked against typical admitted students who boast higher marks across the board. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or context in a new application, and highlighting any personal achievements that showcase your resilience as a Black woman in this field—many schools value diverse stories that predict real-world success beyond numbers. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing.
